texts.shortText:
Medium- to large-sized crab, carapace (hard upper shell) brown to bright orange, with darker middle area; chelae (pincers) orange, walking legs orange-brown.

Carapace 1.5 times wider than long, surface smooth. Front edge with four pairs of short teeth, ‘peg’-like teeth to the sides, with a well-developed tooth lower down. Chelae large and long relative to body size, easily four times carapace width, with 5-6 rows of longitudinal tubercles. As in all swimming crabs, last segment (dactylus) of the fifth leg modified as a flattened ‘paddle’ which it can use to swim through the water column.
Carapace width up to 120 mm, though generally around 55 mm.

Global: Mostly found in the Western Indian Ocean from India South Africa to (and several islands in between), extending all the way into the False Bay.

Local/Regional: From False Bay to Mozambique and beyond, Pelagic species to 450 m.

This fascinating swimming crab is a rare visitor of the Great African Seaforest, as they are predominantly pelagic and usually found in tropical waters. They occasionally venture south when the sea is unusually warm. Interestingly this crab was described from False Bay in 1838 and not seen in South Africa again until 1978 when they were found in astonishing numbers (and again in 1983 and 1993). Known to episodically form huge offshore swarms and then form an important food source for tuna. Commercially important in India and Arabian Sea, where catches are as high as 54000 crabs per hectare (though an average of 500 per hectare is usually caught)!

Like most swimming crabs, they are mobile predators with the largest part of the diet made up of other decapod crustaceans and small fish. Juveniles have a similar diet, which is made up of pelagic shrimps, crabs and foraminiferans, while subadults mostly eat fish (specifically lantern fish) and brachyuran crabs.

Smith’s swimming crabs form dense breeding aggregations, which start on the continental shelf from October to January. Males and females hold each other with their bellies facing each other until the female moults and after which the male transfers a sperm package to the female and both go their own way. Ovigerous females (females with eggs) move deeper and are found on the seafloor where they release their eggs. Larvae usually hatch in shelf waters and subsequently disperse offshore where they are often found in dense swarms. Sexes separate at certain points during the year, and an entire catch may be made up of a single sex.
The uniform orange colouration, large cheliped pincers with rows of turbercles make this species easily distinguishable from the resident Three-spot swimming crab Ovalipes trimaculatus.

Balasubramanian, CP & Suseelan, C (1998). Natural diet of the deep water crab Charybdis smithii McLeay (Brachyura: Portunidae) of the seas around India. Indian Journal of Fisheries, 45(4), 407-412.

Balasubramanian, CP & Suseelan, C (2001). Distribution and population structure of deep-water crab Charybdis smithii (Decapoda: Portunidae) in the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal. Bulletin of Marine Science, 68(3), 435-449.

Barnard, KH (1950). Descriptive Catalogue of South African Decapod Crustacea. Annals of the South African Museum, 38, 1–824.

Branch, G, Griffiths, CL, Branch, M & Beckley, L (2016). Two Oceans: A Guide to the Marine Life of Southern Africa (Fourth edition). Struik Nature, Cape Town. (p. 122).

Emmerson, WD (2016). A Guide to, and Checklist for, the Decapoda of Namibia, South Africa and Mozambique. Newcastle upon Tyne: Cambridge Scholars Publishing. Volumes 1–3, 526, 645, 711 pp.

Losse, GF (1969). Notes on the portunid crab Charybdis edwardsi Leene & Buitendijk, 1949, from the Western Indian Ocean. Journal of Natural History, 3(1), 145-152.
